questions.question: How does CBT conceptualize stress?

options.text: Stress is entirely external — caused by events outside your control
Stress results from the interaction between demands and your perceived ability to cope
Stress is always harmful and should be eliminated completely
Stress is a personality trait — some people are naturally stressed

questions.explanation: Correct. CBT teaches that stress isn't just about the situation — it's about the gap between what's demanded and what you believe you can handle. Two people face the same deadline: one thinks 'I can manage this,' the other thinks 'This is impossible.' The thought creates different stress levels.

questions.question: What are the two main pathways for reducing stress in CBT?

options.text: Avoid all stressful situations and sleep more
Change the situation (problem-solving) and change your response (cognitive/behavioral coping)
Complain to others and wait for stress to pass
Only focus on changing your thoughts — situations don't matter

questions.explanation: Yes. If the situation can be changed (too many commitments → delegate or decline), use problem-solving. If it can't (illness, job loss), use cognitive restructuring and behavioral coping (acceptance, social support, self-care). Most stressful situations require both pathways.

questions.question: Why is cognitive restructuring particularly helpful for stress management?

options.text: Because it eliminates all stressful thoughts permanently
Because it helps you distinguish between real threats and exaggerated worries, reducing unnecessary stress
Because positive thinking makes stressful situations disappear
Because you can ignore problems by changing how you think about them

questions.explanation: Exactly. Much of stress comes from catastrophizing ('This will ruin everything'), mind-reading ('They think I'm incompetent'), or all-or-nothing thinking ('I must be perfect or I've failed'). Cognitive restructuring helps you separate realistic concerns from distorted amplifications.

questions.question: What role does behavioral activation play in stress management?

options.text: It encourages you to work harder and take on more commitments
It helps you maintain valued activities and self-care even when stressed
It teaches you to avoid all stressful activities
Behavioral activation doesn't apply to stress management

questions.explanation: Correct. When stressed, people often abandon exercise, social contact, hobbies — the very things that buffer against stress. Behavioral activation for stress means: 'Even though I'm overwhelmed, I'm going to walk for 20 minutes / call a friend / take a real lunch break.' These activities restore resources.

questions.question: Identify a current source of stress. Is this a problem you can change (problem-solving) or one you need to accept and cope with (cognitive/behavioral)? What specific CBT skill would be most helpful for this stressor and why?
